title: The Bread We Break
summary: The Lord's Supper is not just a personal moment — it is a shared one. The bread we break connects us to Christ and to one another.
suggestedHymns: Blest Be the Tie That Binds
We Are One in the Spirit
bodyText:
Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 10:16–17:

"Is not the cup of thanksgiving for which we give thanks a participation in the blood of Christ? And is not the bread that we break a participation in the body of Christ? Because there is one loaf, we, who are many, are one body, for we all share the one loaf."

There is something about this meal that we must not overlook: it is shared.

Yes, the Lord's Supper is deeply personal. You examine your own heart. You remember your own sin. You receive grace individually.

But you do not take it alone.

Paul says we all share the one loaf. That means when you eat this bread, you are not just connected to Christ — you are connected to every person in this room who belongs to Him.

The same body that was broken for you was broken for the person sitting next to you. The same blood that covers your sin covers theirs.

We are not just individuals who happen to be in the same building. We are one body — bound together by the cross.

So as you take the bread this morning, remember two things: Jesus gave His body for you. And He gave it for us.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Worthy Is the Lamb
summary: Heaven's worship centers on the Lamb who was slain. When we take the Lord's Supper, we join that song.
suggestedHymns: All Hail the Power of Jesus' Name
bodyText:
In Revelation 5, John is given a glimpse into heaven's throne room. And what does he see at the center of it all?

Not a conquering general. Not a shining king on a golden throne.

A Lamb — looking as though it had been slain.

And around that Lamb, a chorus erupts:

"Worthy is the Lamb, who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and strength and honor and glory and praise!" — Revelation 5:12

That is what heaven sounds like. Not past tense worship. Present tense. Ongoing. Eternal. And all of it directed at the One who bled for sinners.

This morning, we gather around a table that echoes that same song.

The bread — His body, given for us.
The cup — His blood, poured out for our sins.

We are not just going through a ritual. We are joining a chorus that stretches from this room to the throne of God.

So take the bread. Take the cup. And let your heart whisper what heaven shouts:

Worthy is the Lamb.

Let's pray.

title: Once for All
summary: The old sacrifices had to be repeated. The sacrifice of Jesus never does. He offered Himself once — and it was enough.
suggestedHymns: It Is Well with My Soul
bodyText:
Under the old covenant, the priests never sat down.

They stood. Day after day. Sacrifice after sacrifice. The blood of bulls and goats could cover sin temporarily — but it could never take it away.

But Hebrews 7:27 says this about Jesus:

"He does not need to offer sacrifices day after day… He sacrificed for their sins once for all when He offered Himself."

Once for all.

Not once for some. Not once for now. Once for all — for every sin, for every sinner, for all time.

And Hebrews 10:10 adds:

"We have been made holy through the sacrifice of the body of Jesus Christ once for all."

That is why we do not come to this table to add anything to His work. We come to remember a sacrifice that is already complete.

The bread — a body offered once.
The cup — blood shed once.

And it was enough.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Blood Speaks
summary: Abel's blood cried out for justice. The blood of Jesus speaks something better — mercy.
suggestedHymns: Are You Washed in the Blood
bodyText:
In Genesis 4, after Cain murdered his brother, God said: "Your brother's blood cries out to Me from the ground."

Abel's blood spoke. And what it said was: Justice. Wrong has been done. Someone must answer for this.

But Hebrews 12:24 tells us that we have come to something greater:

"…to Jesus the mediator of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel."

The blood of Jesus also speaks. But it does not cry out for vengeance.

It speaks mercy.
It speaks forgiveness.
It speaks peace.

Every time we take this cup, we are reminded that blood has been shed — not demanding our punishment, but declaring our pardon.

Abel's blood said, "Someone must pay."
The blood of Christ says, "Someone already has."

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Torn Veil
summary: When Jesus died, the temple veil was torn from top to bottom. The way to God was opened — and it has never been closed.
suggestedHymns: Open My Eyes That I May See
bodyText:
At the moment Jesus breathed His last, something extraordinary happened in the temple.

Matthew 27:51 tells us:

"At that moment the curtain of the temple was torn in two from top to bottom."

That veil had separated the Holy of Holies — the very presence of God — from everyone else. Only the high priest could pass through it, and only once a year.

But when Jesus died, God tore it open. Not from the bottom up — as if a man had done it. From top to bottom. God Himself opened the way.

Hebrews 10:19–22 tells us what that means for us:

"Therefore, brothers and sisters, since we have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by the blood of Jesus… let us draw near to God."

This table is a reminder that because of the cross, the way to God is open. No more barriers. No more distance. No more waiting outside.

The bread — His body, the cost of our access.
The cup — His blood, the key to the Father's presence.

Draw near. The veil is torn.

Let's pray.

title: Reconciled
summary: We were not just forgiven — we were brought back. The cross reconciled us to the God we had walked away from.
bodyText:
There is a difference between being pardoned and being brought home.

A pardon removes a penalty. But reconciliation restores a relationship.

And the cross did both.

Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 5:18–19:

"All this is from God, who reconciled us to Himself through Christ… God was reconciling the world to Himself in Christ, not counting people's sins against them."

Notice who moved first. We did not reconcile ourselves to God. God reconciled us to Himself. We were the ones who walked away. He was the One who came after us.

And notice what reconciliation cost — it came through Christ. Through His body. Through His blood. Through the cross.

So when we take this bread, we remember more than forgiveness. We remember restoration — a broken relationship made whole.

When we drink this cup, we remember that we are not just pardoned criminals. We are welcomed children.

That is what the cross accomplished. Not just removal of guilt — but restoration of fellowship.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: He Carried Our Sorrows
summary: Jesus did not just carry our sins — He carried our grief. The cross was not only about guilt but about the full weight of human pain.
suggestedHymns: What a Friend We Have in Jesus
bodyText:
We often speak of Jesus bearing our sins on the cross. And He did. But Isaiah says He carried something else, too.

"Surely He took up our pain and bore our suffering." — Isaiah 53:4

Not just guilt. Grief.
Not just rebellion. Sorrow.
Not just the penalty of sin — but the weight of it.

Every heartbreak. Every loss. Every tear shed in the dark when no one was watching.

Jesus carried it.

He knows what it feels like to be abandoned by friends. He knows grief — He wept at the grave of Lazarus. He knows rejection, exhaustion, and sorrow "even to the point of death."

So when you come to this table carrying something heavy this morning — and some of you are — know this: the One we remember here understands.

This bread represents a body that bore not only your sin, but your sorrow.
This cup represents blood shed by a Savior who carried your pain to the cross.

You are not alone in what you carry. He carried it first.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: From Death to Life
summary: Because of the cross, we have crossed over from death to life. The Lord's Supper is a celebration of that crossing.
suggestedHymns: My Hope Is Built on Nothing Less
bodyText:
Jesus said something in John 5:24 that should stop us in our tracks:

"Very truly I tell you, whoever hears My word and believes Him who sent Me has eternal life and will not be judged but has crossed over from death to life."

Has crossed over. Past tense.

Not "will cross over someday." Not "might cross over if everything goes well." Has crossed over.

Because of the cross, death is behind us. Life is ahead. And the bridge between the two was built with nails, wood, and the blood of the Son of God.

That is what this table represents.

The bread — a body given so we could live.
The cup — blood poured out so we could cross over.

If you are in Christ this morning, you are not waiting for life. You already have it. The cross secured it. The empty tomb confirmed it.

So take the bread and the cup — not with fear, but with confidence. You have crossed over.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: No Condemnation
summary: For those in Christ, there is no condemnation. Not reduced condemnation. Not temporary relief. None.
suggestedHymns: It Is Well with My Soul
bodyText:
Some of us come to this table carrying guilt. Old guilt. Fresh guilt. The kind that whispers, "You don't really belong here."

To that voice, Paul gives one of the most powerful sentences in Scripture:

"Therefore, there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus." — Romans 8:1

No condemnation.

Not reduced condemnation. Not temporary relief. Not "less condemnation than before."

None.

That word "therefore" points back to everything Paul has just argued — that the law could not save us, that our flesh is weak, that sin is relentless. And yet, in Christ, the verdict has already been handed down: not guilty.

Not because we are innocent. But because Someone took our sentence.

That is why we eat this bread — to remember the body that bore our condemnation.
That is why we drink this cup — to remember the blood that silenced the accusation.

So if guilt followed you to this table today, let it go. It has no authority here. The cross has spoken.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: While We Were Enemies
summary: God did not wait for us to become friendly toward Him. He reconciled us while we were still His enemies.
bodyText:
Paul does not sugarcoat our condition before Christ. He does not call us "seekers" or "spiritually curious." He uses a far harder word:

"For if, while we were God's enemies, we were reconciled to Him through the death of His Son, how much more, having been reconciled, shall we be saved through His life!" — Romans 5:10

Enemies.

Not people who were trying their best. Not people who were almost there. Enemies — hostile toward God, opposed to His will, dead in sin.

And that is when He saved us.

Not after we came around. Not after we cleaned up. While we were enemies.

That changes how we see this table. We did not earn a seat here. We were given one — by a God who loved us when we wanted nothing to do with Him.

This bread — His body, given for enemies.
This cup — His blood, shed for rebels.

And now, by His grace, enemies have become children.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Good Shepherd Lays Down His Life
summary: Jesus is not a hired hand who runs when trouble comes. He is the Good Shepherd who willingly laid down His life for His sheep.
suggestedHymns: Savior Like a Shepherd Lead Us
bodyText:
Jesus said in John 10:11:

"I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down His life for the sheep."

A hired hand runs when the wolf comes. He works for wages, not love. When the cost gets too high, he leaves.

But a shepherd — a true shepherd — stays.

Jesus stayed. When the wolf came — when sin, death, and judgment bore down on His flock — He did not run. He did not negotiate. He stepped between us and destruction.

And He laid down His life.

Notice the language: He laid it down. It was not taken from Him. It was given. Willingly. Deliberately. Lovingly.

"I lay down My life for the sheep… No one takes it from Me, but I lay it down of My own accord." — John 10:15, 18

This bread reminds us of a Shepherd who gave His body for the flock.
This cup reminds us of blood willingly poured out — not under compulsion, but out of love.

You are not forgotten. You are not abandoned. You belong to the Good Shepherd.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Love to the End
summary: Jesus did not love us partially or temporarily. He loved His own to the very end — and the cross proves it.
suggestedHymns: O How He Loves You and Me
bodyText:
John introduces the final night of Jesus' life with a stunning sentence:

"Having loved His own who were in the world, He loved them to the end." — John 13:1

To the end.

Not until they disappointed Him — which they would, that very night.
Not until it became too costly — which it would, within hours.
Not until they proved worthy — which they never did.

To the end.

That phrase carries the weight of everything that followed: the towel and basin, the broken bread, the garden prayer, the arrest, the trial, the cross.

Every step Jesus took that night was love in motion — moving toward the cross, not away from it.

And He did it knowing exactly who He was dying for. Imperfect, unreliable, sometimes faithless people. People like us.

This bread is proof that His love did not stop short.
This cup is proof that His love went all the way.

He loved us to the end. And His love has not ended yet.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: A Living Hope
summary: The resurrection did not just prove that Jesus was alive — it gave us a living hope that can never be taken away.
suggestedHymns: My Hope Is Built on Nothing Less
bodyText:
Peter writes:

"Praise be to the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ! In His great mercy He has given us new birth into a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead." — 1 Peter 1:3

A living hope. Not a fading one. Not a wishful one. A hope that breathes because Christ does.

The world offers plenty of hopes — but they are fragile. A hope built on health fails when the diagnosis comes. A hope built on wealth fails when the market turns. A hope built on people fails when they let you down.

But a hope built on the resurrection? That hope is alive. Because He is alive.

The Lord's Supper points us to the cross — but the cross only means what it means because of the empty tomb. Jesus died. But He did not stay dead. And because He lives, our hope lives too.

This bread — a body that was buried but did not stay in the grave.
This cup — blood that purchased a future death cannot undo.

Take heart this morning. Your hope is not dead. It is risen.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Set Free
summary: The cross did not just forgive our sin — it broke sin's power over us. We are no longer slaves.
bodyText:
Paul writes in Romans 6:6–7:

"For we know that our old self was crucified with Him so that the body ruled by sin might be done away with, that we should no longer be slaves to sin — because anyone who has died has been set free from sin."

The cross did not just pardon us. It liberated us.

There is a difference. A pardon says, "You will not be punished." Freedom says, "You are no longer in chains."

Sin once held us captive. It dictated our habits, our thoughts, our choices. We were enslaved — even when we didn't fully realize it.

But at the cross, those chains were broken. Our old self was crucified with Christ. The power sin once held has been defeated.

That does not mean we never struggle. But it means the struggle is no longer hopeless. We fight from freedom, not toward it.

This bread — a body broken so our bondage could be broken.
This cup — blood shed so slaves could become sons and daughters.

You are free. Live like it.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: He Made Peace
summary: Through the blood of the cross, God made peace between Himself and us. Not a truce — real peace.
suggestedHymns: It Is Well with My Soul
bodyText:
Paul writes in Colossians 1:19–20:

"For God was pleased to have all His fullness dwell in Him, and through Him to reconcile to Himself all things… by making peace through His blood, shed on the cross."

Peace. Not a temporary ceasefire. Not an uneasy truce. Real, lasting peace — made through blood.

Before the cross, we were at war with God. Not always openly. Sometimes quietly. But the hostility was real — our sin stood between us and a holy God like a wall that no human effort could tear down.

And God tore it down Himself — through the blood of His Son.

That is what this cup represents. Not just forgiveness, but peace. The war is over. The debt is settled. The relationship is restored.

And that peace was not cheap — it was purchased at the highest possible price.

So as you take the bread and the cup this morning, let your heart be still. Peace has been made. Not by you — but for you.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Access to the Father
summary: We who were once far away have been brought near by the blood of Christ. We now have access to the Father.
bodyText:
Paul writes to the Ephesians — many of whom were Gentiles, outsiders to the covenant promises:

"But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far away have been brought near by the blood of Christ." — Ephesians 2:13

Far away. That is where we all once stood. Not just geographically or culturally — but spiritually. Separated from God. Without access. Without hope.

But the blood of Christ changed our address.

We were far. Now we are near.
We were strangers. Now we are family.
We were outside. Now we have access.

Ephesians 2:18 says: "For through Him we both have access to the Father by one Spirit."

Access. Not because of our pedigree. Not because of our performance. But because of His blood.

This bread reminds us that His body opened the way.
This cup reminds us that His blood brought us near.

You do not have to earn your way into God's presence. The cross has already brought you there.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Anchor of the Soul
summary: In a world that shifts and shakes, the hope we have in Christ is an anchor — firm and secure, reaching into the very presence of God.
suggestedHymns: My Hope Is Built on Nothing Less
bodyText:
Life is not always steady. Circumstances shift. Health changes. Relationships strain. And sometimes the ground beneath our feet feels like it is moving.

But Hebrews 6:19–20 says this:

"We have this hope as an anchor for the soul, firm and secure. It enters the inner sanctuary behind the curtain, where our forerunner, Jesus, has entered on our behalf."

An anchor does not stop the storm. But it keeps you from drifting.

And our anchor is not a philosophy. Not a principle. It is a Person — Jesus, who has already entered the presence of God on our behalf.

He is there now. Seated. Interceding. Holding us fast.

When we take the Lord's Supper, we are gripping that anchor. We are reminding our hearts that no matter what this week has brought — or what next week may bring — we are tethered to something that cannot be moved.

This bread — His body, the cost of our security.
This cup — His blood, the guarantee of our hope.

Hold on. The anchor holds.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Fragrance of the Offering
summary: Christ's sacrifice was not just sufficient — it was pleasing to God. He gave Himself as a fragrant offering.
suggestedHymns: When I Survey the Wondrous Cross
Take My Life and Let It Be
bodyText:
Paul writes in Ephesians 5:2:

"Walk in the way of love, just as Christ loved us and gave Himself up for us as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God."

A fragrant offering. That language comes from the Old Testament sacrificial system, where the aroma of certain offerings was described as "pleasing to the Lord."

And here Paul applies that language to the cross.

The sacrifice of Jesus was not just necessary — it was beautiful to God. Not because God delights in suffering, but because the offering represented perfect obedience, perfect love, and perfect surrender.

Jesus gave Himself willingly. Completely. Lovingly. And the Father received that offering with approval.

So this morning, as we take the bread and the cup, we are remembering something that was not only costly — it was precious. It was the one offering that fully satisfied the justice and the love of God.

This bread — a body given as a sweet aroma before the throne.
This cup — blood poured out in perfect, pleasing sacrifice.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Not Ashamed
summary: The message of the cross may seem foolish to the world, but it is the power of God for salvation. We are not ashamed of it.
suggestedHymns: I'm Not Ashamed to Own My Lord
Stand Up, Stand Up for Jesus
bodyText:
Paul declares in Romans 1:16:

"For I am not ashamed of the gospel, because it is the power of God that brings salvation to everyone who believes."

The world has never known quite what to do with the cross. To some, it looks like weakness. To others, foolishness. A crucified Savior is not what the world expects — or wants.

But Paul says he is not ashamed. Why?

Because the gospel works. It does not merely inform — it transforms. It does not merely teach — it saves. It is power.

And that power flows from the very event we remember at this table: the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ.

This bread represents a body that the world despised — but God used to save.
This cup represents blood that the world dismissed — but heaven honored.

We are not ashamed of this message. We are not ashamed of this table. Because the cross that the world calls foolish is the wisest, most powerful act in all of history.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Grace at the Table
summary: We do not come to this table because we earned a seat. We come because grace pulled out the chair. Salvation is a gift — and this meal is a reminder of it.
bodyText:
Paul writes in Ephesians 2:8–9:

"For it is by grace you have been saved, through faith — and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God — not by works, so that no one can boast."

Gift. That word should change how we approach this table.

We did not earn a place here. We did not qualify. We did not pass a test or meet a threshold. We were given a seat — by grace.

Grace means that God moved toward us before we moved toward Him. Grace means the price was paid before we knew we owed it. Grace means we are welcomed not because of who we are, but because of what Christ has done.

Some of us come to this table feeling unworthy. And in one sense, that is exactly right — none of us are worthy of what happened on the cross. But unworthiness is not a reason to stay away. It is the very reason grace exists.

This bread — a gift of broken body, given freely.
This cup — a gift of shed blood, poured out for the undeserving.

You did not earn this. You cannot repay it. You can only receive it — and give thanks.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.
